title: Making an altered book journal
subtitle: Transforming an existing hardback with PVA, gesso, and page-sealing to create an art journal
excerpt: An altered book takes a found hardback and transforms it into a mixed-media journal: pages are sealed into thick painting panels with PVA, selected spreads are primed with gesso, and windows or tunnels are cut through sealed page blocks. The tutorial covers the physical preparation from cover selection to gessoed surface.
sourceNotes: Altered book practice developed as a mixed-media art form from the 1990s onward. No pre-1928 source applies. The techniques involved (gesso priming, PVA page sealing, window cutting) are derived from general bookbinding and mixed-media craft principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: An acrylic-based white primer. Applied to a book page, it seals the printed text and provides a matte, slightly absorbent surface that accepts paint, ink, and other wet media.

The process of applying PVA adhesive to the outer edges of a stack of pages while they are held together, binding them into a single rigid block. The resulting block is thick enough to work on without the pages shifting or tearing.
The bound pages of a book as a unit, distinct from the covers. In an altered book, sections of the text block are sealed together or removed to create panels and voids.

content.text: An altered book starts with a found hardback. The printed content of the original becomes a layer beneath whatever is worked on top. The pages are not blank; they carry text, images, columns, and margins that show through thin paint and add visual complexity. Preparation involves three main steps: sealing groups of pages into solid blocks thick enough to paint on without tearing, priming selected spreads with
gesso
, and optionally cutting windows or tunnels through sealed
text-block
sections.

attrs.title: Cutting tools and adhesives
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones: a blunt blade skids. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut against a metal ruler with the non-cutting hand at least two finger-widths back from the blade. PVA and gesso are safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Clean brushes immediately after use with warm water: dried PVA sets hard in bristles and ruins brushes.

items.name: Found hardback book, thick, non-glossy pages
items.substitutions: Old encyclopaedia volumes, textbooks, and novels all work well. Avoid coffee-table books with glossy photographic paper: PVA does not bond glossy surfaces well and gesso will bead up rather than soak in.

items.name: PVA, regular
items.substitutions: Acid-free PVA gives a longer-lasting result, but regular PVA works for experimental and short-term journals.

items.name: White gesso
items.substitutions: Coloured gesso works as a base; clear gesso lets the underlying text show through while sealing the surface.

items.name: Paste brush, 50 mm flat
items.qty: 1 dedicated brush for PVA and 1 for gesso
items.substitutions: A cheap household paintbrush works. Keep PVA and gesso brushes separate.

items.name: Palette knife, small
items.substitutions: A stiff card scrap for spreading thick gesso also works.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, for paper additions
Washi tape
items.qty: Optional, for page decoration and hinge reinforcement

content.text: Choosing the book

A thick hardback (300 pages or more) gives more material to work with. Non-glossy pages absorb PVA and gesso readily. The original subject matter of the book is visible through thin paint layers and becomes part of the visual texture: an encyclopaedia page showing plant diagrams reads differently as a background than a dense column of text. Look at the actual pages before choosing: some books have pleasant typesetting, interesting illustrations, or useful visual elements that complement the painted work.
Sealing page blocks
Individual book pages are too thin and fragile to work on directly with wet media. Sealing a group of pages together creates a
page seal
: a thick, rigid working surface. A sealed block of 10-20 pages behaves like a sheet of thick card.

Decide which spreads you want to work on. Choose every third or fourth spread across the book so you have several working surfaces distributed through the volume. The pages between your working spreads will be sealed into blocks.

Open the book to the first spread you want to preserve. Brush a thin layer of PVA onto the top edge of the page block behind it (the pages that will become the first sealed block). Work the PVA into the edges of the pages from top to bottom while holding the pages tightly together. The PVA seeps between the pages and bonds them.
Apply PVA to the foredge and bottom edge of the same block in the same way. Leave the spine edge unsealed: the sealed block must remain attached to the binding.

Place a piece of waxed paper or baking paper inside the spread you want to preserve, between the sealed block and the next working spread. Close the book and press it under a heavy weight for at least two hours. The waxed paper prevents the sealed block sticking to the spread above it.
Repeat for each block between your chosen working spreads. The book dries with several rigid sealed panels distributed through it, separated by the working spreads.
Priming spreads with gesso

Each working spread needs at least two coats of gesso before painting. The first coat is thin (diluted slightly with water) and applied broadly with the paste brush, covering the full spread from edge to edge including the inside spine margin. This seals the paper surface and prevents subsequent paint layers from bleeding through. Allow it to dry fully (15-20 minutes at room temperature).

The second coat is thicker and applied with the palette knife as well as the brush. Use the palette knife to spread gesso in broad sweeps, leaving some texture on the surface: this tooth provides grip for subsequent layers. The palette knife also allows you to build up raised texture in specific areas if you want three-dimensional surface variation. Allow to dry fully before working over the top.
Cutting windows and tunnels

A window is a rectangular or shaped cut-out through a sealed page block that reveals the spread beneath it. A tunnel is a deeper cut through multiple sealed blocks. Both are cut with the craft knife after the block is fully dry and sealed.

To cut a window: open the book to the sealed block you want to cut into. Lay the cutting mat between the sealed block and the spread beneath it. Mark the window shape on the top face of the sealed block in pencil. Cut through the full depth of the block against a metal ruler using repeated light passes rather than one heavy cut. Remove the cut section cleanly. The window reveals whatever is on the spread below, which may be printed text, an illustration, or a gessoed working surface.
Adding paper inserts

Additional pages of cartridge paper can be attached to the inside faces of sealed blocks with PVA to extend the working surface area. Brush a thin layer of PVA onto the back of the cartridge sheet and smooth it onto the face of the sealed block. Press under baking paper and a heavy book until dry. The cartridge sheet then becomes part of the gessoed working surface for the spread.
Care and storage

Store the altered book closed and flat, not upright on a shelf: the uneven thickness of sealed blocks creates a wedge shape that stresses the spine when stored vertically. If the book cover becomes warped from repeated wetting with gesso and paint, press it between boards under a weight overnight to flatten it again. The hardback cover tolerates damp far better than a paperback.
Variations

Coloured or tinted gesso shifts the tonal character of the working surface. Black gesso on a spread creates a dark ground that changes the character of every colour applied over it. Clear gesso preserves more of the underlying text and image as a visible layer through subsequent paint. The degree to which the original text shows through is a variable you control by how many gesso coats you apply and how thickly.
